headline: PepsiCo's sales jump after it cuts prices
summary: PepsiCo's decision to cut prices and some new snacks boosted demand in the first quarter.  Revenue jumped 8.5% to $19.44 billion in the January-March period compared to the same period a year ago, the Purchase, New York, company said Thursday.  PepsiCo began cutting prices on value brands like Chester's and Santitas last spring to win back customers exasperated by years of price hikes.

Revenue jumped 8.5% to $19.44 billion in the January-March period compared to the same period a year ago, the Purchase, New York, company said Thursday.  PepsiCo began cutting prices on value brands like Chester's and Santitas last spring to win back customers exasperated by years of price hikes.

headline: 150 Million Americans Feel Dehydrated*. Gatorade Aims to Change How People Think About Hydration.
summary: PepsiCo (NASDAQ: PEP) today announced a transformational new era for the Gatorade brand, expanding how its science-backed hydration expertise shows up beyond sport occasions. Built on 60 years of research studying hydration's effects on athlete performance, Gatorade remains committed to that purpose and is applying the same rigor to help people better understand and meet their hydration needs across a broader range of activities and moments. Gatorade's new era includes a refreshed product label

headline: Gatorade, inventor of the sports drink, is getting a rebrand targeting non-athletes
summary: Sixty years after it invented sports drinks, Gatorade is making a surprising pivot: It’s no longer focusing primarily on athletes.  PepsiCo, Gatorade’s parent company, said Thursday that the brand wants to broaden its reach to non-athletes who are looking for ways to hydrate, whether they’re on a long flight, going for a walk or nursing a hangover.  The change reflects U.S. consumers’ booming interest in beverages with perceived health benefits.

content.title: Gatorade, inventor of the sports drink, is getting a rebrand targeting non-athletes
content.summary: Sixty years after it invented sports drinks, Gatorade is making a surprising pivot: It’s no longer focusing primarily on athletes.  PepsiCo, Gatorade’s parent company, said Thursday that the brand wants to broaden its reach to non-athletes who are looking for ways to hydrate, whether they’re on a long flight, going for a walk or nursing a hangover.  The change reflects U.S. consumers’ booming interest in beverages with perceived health benefits.

thumbnail.caption: Athletics' Lawrence Butler, left, and Max Muncy dump Gatorade on Brent Rooker, right, after he hit a walk-off three-run home run during the tenth inning of a baseball game against the Houston Astros Sunday, April 5, 2026, in West Sacramento, Calif. (AP Photo/Sara Nevis)

headline: PepsiCo (NASDAQ:PEP) Surprises With Q1 CY2026 Sales
summary: Food and beverage company PepsiCo (NASDAQ:PEP) reported Q1 CY2026 results beating Wall Street’s revenue expectations, with sales up 8.5% year on year to $19.44 billion. Its non-GAAP profit of $1.61 per share was 3.8% above analysts’ consensus estimates.

content.title: PepsiCo (NASDAQ:PEP) Surprises With Q1 CY2026 Sales

headline: PepsiCo price cuts drive Q1 earnings. Analyst asks if that'll be enough.
summary: PepsiCo (PEP) topped first quarter earnings and revenue estimates, attributed to price cuts across its various brands as snackers ate up these more affordable options, namely $7 bags of Doritos. RBC Capital Markets managing director Nik Modi breaks down how PepsiCo is dealing with inflation pressures — including the ripple effect from energy price shocks tied to the Iran war — and what it could mean for both consumer and investor sentiment moving forward.

content.title: PepsiCo price cuts drive Q1 earnings. Analyst asks if that'll be enough.
content.description:
PepsiCo (PEP) topped first quarter earnings and revenue estimates, attributed to price cuts across its various brands as snackers ate up these more affordable options, namely $7 bags of Doritos.

RBC Capital Markets managing director Nik Modi breaks down how PepsiCo is dealing with inflation pressures — including the ripple effect from energy price shocks tied to the Iran war — and what it could mean for both consumer and investor sentiment moving forward.
content.summary: PepsiCo (PEP) topped first quarter earnings and revenue estimates, attributed to price cuts across its various brands as snackers ate up these more affordable options, namely $7 bags of Doritos. RBC Capital Markets managing director Nik Modi breaks down how PepsiCo is dealing with inflation pressures — including the ripple effect from energy price shocks tied to the Iran war — and what it could mean for both consumer and investor sentiment moving forward.

headline: ‘Inflation Will Come’ From Iran War, PepsiCo Says
summary: PepsiCo sees the Iran war driving another round of higher prices for consumers.  “Our assumption is that inflation will come,” CFO Steve Schmitt said on an investor call Thursday, though he said it is too soon to know how much.  PepsiCo said it has not had any supply chain issues tied to the Iran war, and that the food and beverage giant maintains six- to 12-month hedges for ingredients and commodities like oats, corn and aluminum.

content.title: ‘Inflation Will Come’ From Iran War, PepsiCo Says
content.summary: PepsiCo sees the Iran war driving another round of higher prices for consumers.  “Our assumption is that inflation will come,” CFO Steve Schmitt said on an investor call Thursday, though he said it is too soon to know how much.  PepsiCo said it has not had any supply chain issues tied to the Iran war, and that the food and beverage giant maintains six- to 12-month hedges for ingredients and commodities like oats, corn and aluminum.
